answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to the elbow?
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any activities or movements that make the elbow pain worse or better?
answers.intake_a5: Tennis makes it worse and rest makes it better
answers.intake_q6: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the elbow pain so far?
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms?
answers.intake_q9: Have you noticed any clicking, locking, or decreased range of motion in your elbow?
answers.intake_q10: Have you ever been diagnosed with tennis elbow or any other elbow condition before?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain located on the outside of her elbow. She reports no recent injury or trauma and denies swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow. The pain worsens with playing tennis and improves with rest. She has not experienced numbness, tingling, weakness, fever, chills, or systemic symptoms. She has not noticed any clicking, locking, or decreased range of motion. She has not previously been diagnosed with tennis elbow or any other elbow condition. Initial treatment included icing on the first day. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain exacerbated by activity involving wrist extension and forearm supination, without signs of infection or neurologic compromise. 

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Topical NSAIDs or acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; avoid in patients with known allergies to NSAIDs. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if there is worsening pain, development of swelling, redness, warmth, fever, numbness, tingling, weakness, or decreased range of motion. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time unless symptoms worsen or fail to improve with conservative management.

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis | RA | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: joints. RA affects people differently. You might have the disease for only a short time, or your symptoms might come and go. The severe form can be chronic (long-lasting) and may last a lifetime. Symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis may include: Joint pain or stiffness when moving. This is usually worse in the morning or after inactivity. RA usually affects both…
